result: I keep returning to the idea that our thoughts are migratory birds: they land inside us briefly, rest, feed, then lift off again toward distant weather. Yesterday morning, while rinsing coffee grounds from the filter, the phrase “no permanence is ours” circled the kitchen like a swallow testing rafters. I don’t know who first spoke it—maybe Rilke, maybe the room itself—but it hummed so vibrantly that I let the water run cold while I listened. In that dilated minute, every goodbye I’ve ever postponed gathered at the window, pecking glass, asking to be released. So I opened it. They spilled out as rust-colored silhouettes against a sky the exact shade of overdue letters. I felt both lighter and more porous, as though a wind were sweeping through empty apartments in my ribs, rearranging the curtains. Mid-afternoon, a cloud shaped like my childhood cat drifted over the park, pawing at invisible yarn; strangers looked up, puzzled by their own sudden tenderness. Maybe memory isn’t stored inside us at all but wanders overhead like flocks that darken the sun for instants before continuing on. All we can do is lie in the grass, keep our eyes open, and note how silence, for a second, resembles a ceiling lowered by angels. Later, the birds descend into unknown hemispheres; we fold the laundry. Still, something migratory remains—an echo of wings behind the heartbeat—promising return amid ordinary rinsing, rinsing of cups, of days, of our unclaimed, ever-moving selves.
